coredata.dc:title: DNA assembly of carbon dots and 5-fluorouracil used for room-temperature phosphorescence turn-on sensing of AFP and AFP-triggered simultaneous release of dual-drug
coredata.dc:creator: Gui, Rijun
He, Weijie
Jin, Hui
Sun, Jie
Wang, Yanfeng
coredata.dc:description: In this article, carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized via hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and ethylenediamine, and modified on the surface to generate HOOC-CDs. 3′-NH2-terminated capture ssDNA (DNA1) combined with HOOC-CDs to prepare CDs-DNA1 complex through carbodiimide bonding. Due to the specific DNA complementary combination of DNA1 and DNA2, CDs-DNA1 combined with target ssDNA (DNA2) labelled with 5-fluorouracil (5Fu) at the 5′-end to generate CDs-5Fu DNA assembly. After loading of glucosamine (Glu) into double-strands complementary DNA, CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid as a versatile carrier was synthesized. DNA2 in the hybrid served as an aptamer of alpha fetal protein (AFP) and specifically combined with AFP, which could trigger the unwinding of CDs-DNA1 and 5Fu-DNA2, and simultaneous release of 5Fu and Glu. In the hybrid, 5Fu induced room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) quenching of CDs owing to photoinduced electron transfer from CDs to 5Fu. The addition of ATP resulted in the separation (drug release) of 5Fu and Glu from CDs, accompanied by RTP recovery of CDs. Experimental results verified that CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid could be developed towards a novel RTP turn-on probe for AFP detection and an advanced carrier for AFP-triggered simultaneous release of 5Fu and Glu.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
5-fluorouracil
Phosphorescence
Alpha fetal protein
Drug release

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs-5Fu DNA assembly and CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid
AFP detection experiments in real samples
Preparation and characterization of HOOC-CDs and CDs-DNA1
Absorption, FL and RTP spectrum properties of products
RTP spectrum responses and mechanism of products
RTP turn-on probe of AFP and AFP-triggered drug release

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation and application procedures of CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid.
(a) TEM and inserted HRTEM images of the prepared HOOC-CDs. (b) Raman spectrum of HOOC-CDs. (c) XPS spectra of HOOC-CDs: the whole survey and inserted C1s. (d) FTIR spectra of HOOC-CDs and CDs-DNA1.

(a) UV absorption spectra of DNA1, HOOC-CDs, CDs-DNA1, CDs-5Fu and CDs-5Fu-Glu. (b) FL and RTP spectra of 5Fu, CDs, human serum and their mixture. As for RTP, delay time is 0.2ms, gate time is 0.4ms, and cycle time is 20ms. (c) RTP spectra of CDs-DNA1, CDs-5Fu, CDs-5Fu-Glu without and with the addition of AFP. (d) RTP emission spectra of CDs-5Fu-Glu in the presence of AFP. (e) Linear plotting of relative RTP peak intensity versus Log [AFP]. (f) Cumulative 5Fu and Glu release profiles from CDs-5Fu-Glu in 1mM PBS (pH 6.5) with the addition of AFP (178μM). The inserts are the dynamic RTP responses.

(a, b) Relative RTP peak intensities (I/I 0) of CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid dispersed in PBS with the additions of AFP (178μM), tumor biomarkers, ions and biomolecules (1.78mM), and the mixture containing all interferents (26.7mM). (c) The viabilities of HeLa cells incubated with 1.0mgmL−1 of CDs-5Fu and CDs-5Fu-Glu before and after the addition of AFP (178μM).

head.ce:title: DNA assembly of carbon dots and 5-fluorouracil used for room-temperature phosphorescence turn-on sensing of AFP and AFP-triggered simultaneous release of dual-drug

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Laboratory of Fiber Materials and Modern Textile, The Growing Base for State Key Laboratory, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071,PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Laboratory of Fiber Materials and Modern Textile
The Growing Base for State Key Laboratory

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materia Medica, Shandong Academy of Medical Sciences, Jinan, 250062, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized via hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and ethylenediamine, and modified on the surface to generate HOOC-CDs. 3′-NH2-terminated capture ssDNA (DNA1) combined with HOOC-CDs to prepare CDs-DNA1 complex through carbodiimide bonding. Due to the specific DNA complementary combination of DNA1 and DNA2, CDs-DNA1 combined with target ssDNA (DNA2) labelled with 5-fluorouracil (5Fu) at the 5′-end to generate CDs-5Fu DNA assembly. After loading of glucosamine (Glu) into double-strands complementary DNA, CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid as a versatile carrier was synthesized. DNA2 in the hybrid served as an aptamer of alpha fetal protein (AFP) and specifically combined with AFP, which could trigger the unwinding of CDs-DNA1 and 5Fu-DNA2, and simultaneous release of 5Fu and Glu. In the hybrid, 5Fu induced room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) quenching of CDs owing to photoinduced electron transfer from CDs to 5Fu. The addition of ATP resulted in the separation (drug release) of 5Fu and Glu from CDs, accompanied by RTP recovery of CDs. Experimental results verified that CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid could be developed towards a novel RTP turn-on probe for AFP detection and an advanced carrier for AFP-triggered simultaneous release of 5Fu and Glu.

ce:section.ce:para: As an important category of carbon materials, carbon dots (CDs) have received much attention in recent years because CDs have abundant advantages such as low toxicity, facile preparation, highly colloidal and photochemical stability. In addition, CDs possess a broad range of significant applications especially in sensing and imaging researches [1]. Previous papers mainly focus on the research relative to the fluorescence of CDs, but pay less attention to the phosphorescence of CDs [2–10]. In general, phosphorescence has several unique merits over fluorescence, including long emission lifetime, wide band-gap between excitation and emission spectra, weak interference from short-lived autofluorescence, background fluorescence and scattering light. According to a longer emission lifetime allowing for a shorter time delay, phosphorescence at room temperature (RTP) is considered as time-resolved luminescence. The use of RTP easily avoids short-lived fluorescence and scattering light. So far, RTP has been extensively utilized as an ideal mode for bio-sensing and bio-imaging applications [2].

In 2013, the RTP of CDs was discovered firstly. CDs were embedded into poly(vinyl alcohol) matrix to fabricate a complex that possessed a longer RTP lifetime about ∼380ms [3]. The RTP was observed from CDs dispersed into KAl(SO4)2·xH2O and polyurethane [4,5]. RTP of N-doped CDs and Fe3+-engineered CDs were also confirmed [6,7]. Moreover, the RTP of CDs was further exploited for various sensing applications, such as the sensing of Hg2+, Fe3+, DNA, adenosine-5′- triphosphate and thrombin [2,6,8,9]. Owing to the superior properties from CDs and RTP, a further exploitation of CDs’ RTP for expanded and significant applications is still highly required, and it currently becomes a challenging research.

In this article, we developed a novel application of CDs’ RTP for both targeted bio-sensing and simultaneous controlled release of dual-drug based on a smart CDs-based hybrid. Phosphorescent CDs were prepared via hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and ethylenediamine, and modified on the surface to generate carboxyl-functionalized CDs (HOOC-CDs) [2,10]. Then, HOOC-CDs combined with 3′-NH2-terminated capture ssDNA (DNA1) to form CDs-DNA1 complex through carbodiimide bonding [11]. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , CDs-DNA1 combined with target ssDNA (DNA2) labelled with 5-fluorouracil (5Fu) at 5′-end to fabricate CDs-5Fu DNA assembly, based on the specific complementary combination of double-strands DNA (DNA1 and DNA2, accurate base-pair matching). Then, glucosamine (Glu) was loaded into the complementary DNA double- strands of CDs-5Fu DNA assembly by insert effects to form CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid. As DNA2 was prior designed as the aptamer of alpha fetal protein (AFP), DNA2 could specifically combine with AFP. So, the addition of AFP would trigger the unwinding of CDs-DNA1 and 5Fu-DNA2, together with simultaneous separation (release) of 5Fu and Glu from CDs.

In CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid, the combination of 5Fu and CDs could result in the RTP quenching of CDs (RTP-off) due to the photoinduced electron transfer (PIET) from CDs to 5Fu. As established, CDs acted as an electron donor and 5Fu was used for an efficient electron acceptor [12–14]. Glu contains active functional groups (e.g. −NH2, −OH) similar to doxorubicin [2,15] and can be loaded into the complementary DNA double-strands of CDs-5Fu DNA assembly through insert effects. The as-proposed hybrid could serve as a versatile carrier that loaded the dual-drug of 5Fu and Glu. In the presence of AFP, both 5Fu and Glu would be released simultaneously, because the specific combination of DNA2 with AFP induced the unwinding of CDs-DNA1 and 5Fu-DNA2. Generally, the combination of one biomolecule (AFP) with its aptamer (artificial oligonucleotides) possesses high specificity, selectivity and affinity. Thus, AFP-triggered dual-release of 5Fu and Glu from the hybrid was highly specific. The release of 5Fu meant the separation of 5Fu away from CDs, which could restrain the process of PIET and lead to the RTP recovery (RTP-on) of CDs reasonably. The RTP-on responses of CDs was dependent on AFP. The simultaneous dual-drug (5Fu, Glu) release from the hybrid was also triggered by AFP. Consequently, the CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid could serve as a novel and versatile carrier that realized both the RTP turn-on sensing of AFP and AFP-triggered simultaneous release of dual-drug.

Citric acid, ethylenediamine, 5Fu and Glu were obtained from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China. Cysteine, histidine, glucose, glutathione, uric acid, thiohydracrylic acid, ascorbic acid, N-hydroxysuccinimide sodium (NHS) and 1-ethyl-(3-(3-dimethylamino)propyl) carboxylate hydrochloride (EDC) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. AFP, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), CA125 antigen, squamous cell carcinoma related antigen (SCC), prostate specific antigen (PSA), neuron specific enolase (NSE), human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG) and DNA oligonucleotides (100μM) were bought from Shanghai Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd., China. The base sequences and structures of DNA oligonucleotides are listed as follows: Capture ssDNA (DNA1): 5′-GGCA GGAA GACA AACA AGCT TGGC GGCG GGAA GGTG TTTA AATT CCCG GGTC TGCG TGGT CTGT GGTG CTGT-NH2-3′. Target ssDNA (DNA2 labelled with 5Fu at the 5′-end, with a perfect matching with DNA1): 3′-CCGT CCTT CTGT TTGT TCGA ACCG CCGC CCTT CCAC AAAT TTAA GGGC CCAG ACGC ACCA GACA CCAC GACA-5Fu-5′. Other chemicals were bought from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China. All chemicals could be directly used as received without further purification and ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm−1 (Millipore Simplicity, USA) was used in experiments.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were acquired with a JEOL JEM-1400 TEM operating at 120kV of acceleration voltage (JEOL, Japan). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained from an H-600 (Hitachi, Japan) TEM operating at 200kV of acceleration voltage. Absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on the transmission mode with a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). Fluorescence (FL) emission spectra were measured with a FLSP 920 FL spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instrument, UK). Phosphorescence spectra were recorded on an LS-55 FL spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA) equipped with a quartz cell (1cm×1cm) in the phosphorescence mode. Time-resolved RTP and FL decay curves were detected on a FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba, France). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific) was captured by using a Kratos XSAM 800 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Hydrodynamic diameters were determined by a dynamic light scattering apparatus (DLS, Malven Instruments, UK).

Briefly, 2.5g of citric acid and 1.5mL of ethylenediamine were dissolved in 25mL of ultrapure water and adequately mixed to prepare a homogeneous mixture under agitation. Then, the mixture was transferred into a 50mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 150°C for 6h. The reaction products were obtained and treated by dialyzing against ultrapure water (retained molecular weight: 3.5kDa) frequently (12 times in 24h) to purify the products of CDs. The aqueous suspension of CDs in dialysis bag was treated through circumrotate evaporation and precipitated with acetone. The precipitates were collected by centrifugation and then washed with ethanol repeatedly. After three repeated post-treatments, the ultimate precipitates were collected and dried in vacuum at 60°C to achieve the dry powder of CDs. After that, 50mg of CDs was dispersed in 50mL of aqueous solution containing 2.5g of NaOH and 2.5g of ClCH2COONa, followed by sonication treatment for 3h in water bath. The obtained NaOOC-CDs was neutralized with HCl and dialyzed to obtain HOOC-CDs for further use.

Typically, 10mg of HOOC-CDs was dispersed in 15mL of phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 1mM, pH 6.5) to prepare a homogenous mixture under ultrasonication and agitation. Afterward, 50mg of succinic anhydride was added in the mixture, allowing for continuous reaction of 2h. The reaction solution was centrifuged and washed with PBS repeatedly. Then, 3mg of EDC and 4.5mg of NHS dispersed in 7.5mL of Tris-HCl buffer (1mM, pH 6.5) containing 2mM of NaOH were added in the reaction solution, keeping an additional reaction of 30min under stirring. After that, 50μL of DNA1 (1μM) was added and the reaction solution was incubated for 12h at 37°C. The excess HOOC-CDs was removed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 7kDa) against ultrapure water frequently. The resulting products were centrifuged, washed with Tris-HCl buffer and dried to achieve the purified CDs-DNA1 complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs-5Fu DNA assembly and CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: 5Fu-labelled DNA2 (50μL, 1μM) was dispersed in aqueous suspension of CDs-DNA1 (1.0mgmL−1) to form a mixture. After a continuous incubation of the mixture in the dark for 16h at room temperature, the combination of CDs-DNA1 with 5Fu-DNA2 occurred to fabricate CDs-5Fu DNA assembly. The precipitates of products were collected by centrifugation, washed with ethanol, and dried in vacuum at 37°C to obtain purified CDs-5Fu DNA assembly. Afterward, Glu was added in aqueous suspension of the assembly in PBS (1mM, pH 6.5). The final concentration of Glu was fixed to 1.0mgmL−1. After a continuous incubation of the mixture solution in the dark for 12h, the CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid was achieved. The excess Glu was removed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3.5kDa) against water frequently. The products were centrifuged, washed with PBS and dried to achieve the purified CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid.

Potential interferents from other tumor biomarkers were studied, such as CEA, CA125 antigen, SCC, PSA, NSE and HCG. Potential interferents from human body fluids including biomolecules (cysteine, histidine, glucose, glutathione, uric acid, ascorbic acid, thiohydracrylic acid) and ions (PO4 3−, CO3 2−, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+) were selected to evaluate their effects on RTP responses of CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid, compared with AFP. The interferents were added in 1mM PBS (pH 6.5) containing 1.0mgmL−1 of hybrid to prepare a group of mixture solutions. The final concentrations of the each interferent and AFP were respectively fixed to 1.78mM and 178μM. The mixture consisting of all interferents had a concentration of 26.7mM. RTP spectra of all mixture solutions were measured. The I/I 0 values were calculated and used to evaluate RTP intensity responses of hybrid before (I 0) and after (I) the addition of AFP or interferents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AFP detection experiments in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Real human serum samples from healthy volunteers were selected to investigate the capacity of AFP detection based on the hybrid-based RTP probe. The each group of real samples was diluted by 50-fold with PBS (1mM, pH 6.5) to prepare the PBS-sample solution. Afterward, the aqueous suspension of the hybrid (probe) was added in PBS-sample solutions to prepare the probe-sample mixtures, followed by the addition of AFP. The final concentration of hybrid was fixed to 1.0mgmL−1, while the final concentration of AFP [AFP] was regulated to 10nM, 100nM, 1μM, 10μM and 100μM, respectively. RTP spectra of probe-sample mixtures were measured and the values of [AFP] were calculated through the plotting linear curve of I/I 0 versus [AFP].

Cytotoxicity experiments of CDs-5Fu DNA assembly and CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid were conducted by a standard MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-dipheny-ltetrazolium bromide) assay. HeLa cells were cultured in the Dulbecco’s modified eagle medium (DMEM) containing 10% of fetal bovine serum, 100 units mL−1 of penicillin and 100mgmL−1 of streptomycin in a humidified CO2 (5%) atmosphere at 37°C. HeLa cells were seeded in 96-well plate at a density of 1×104 cells/well and cultured for 24h prior to assay in 5% CO2 at 37°C. After the removing of DMEM medium, the cells were exposed to CDs-5Fu and CDs-5Fu-Glu (the final concentrations of both samples in cell medium were 1.0mgmL−1, with the addition of 178μM AFP). As a reference, the CDs-5Fu without the addition of AFP was directly dispersed in DMEM medium. The above three groups of samples and HeLa cells without the addition of samples (as a control) were incubated for 0∼180min in the dark at 37°C. The incubated solutions were removed and washed with PBS for three times. Then, 100mL of MTT solution (0.5mgmL−1) was added in the each well and incubated for 6h. The medium was replaced with 100mL of dimethyl sulfoxide. The absorbance was monitored at 570nm, by using a Versamax microplate reader. Detected results were quantified by manually subtracting the blank value and normalized against the control value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of HOOC-CDs and CDs-DNA1
ce:section.ce:para: In the preparation of CDs, hydrothermal reaction of citric acid and ethylenediamine led to the formation of polymer-like CDs. After a further carbonization to remove H2O at 150°C, carbogenic CDs were gained and modified on the surface to form HOOC-functionalized CDs (HOOC-CDs). The as-prepared HOOC-CDs were embedded into the polymer-like matrix from amide coupling of citric acid with ethylenediamine. In Fig. 1 a, TEM images of HOOC-CDs demonstrated the almost monodisperse nanoparticles that had a spherical morphology and a nearly uniform diameter. The average diameter of HOOC-CDs was about 12.5±1.3nm. As inserted in Fig. 1a, HRTEM images of HOOC-CDs had a clear interlayer spacing of ∼0.21nm, which was assigned to the d-spacing of graphene (1-100) planes of CDs. In Fig. 1b, Raman spectrum of HOOC-CDs showed two bands at ∼1575 and 1365cm−1, which respectively corresponded to G-band (sp2) and D-band (sp3). Usually, G-band and D-band are relative to the vibration modes of a sp2 carbon atom and a carbon atom in sp3 hybridized orbital, respectively. The ratio of order carbon and disorder carbon was ∼1.6: 1 (G: D), based on the Raman peak intensities at G-band and D-band. XPS spectrum of HOOC-CDs in Fig. 1c showed three peaks at 285, 399 and 532eV, which were corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. As inserted in Fig. 1c, C1s spectrum indicated the main three types of carbon bonds: C-C (284.6eV), C-N (286.1eV) and C-O (287.7eV). After amide coupling of HOOC-CDs with 3′-NH2-terminated DNA1, CDs-DNA1 was prepared and characterized by FTIR spectra. In Fig. 1d, absorption bands at ∼3400, 1650 and 1240cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of O-H, CO and C-OH, indicating −COOH on the surface of CDs. The characteristic absorption bands of −NH, −NCO and NHCO occurred at ∼3100, 1560 and 1240cm−1, respectively. These results verified the combination of CDs and DNA1 through amide coupling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption, FL and RTP spectrum properties of products
ce:section.ce:para: UV absorption spectra of different samples were researched. When compared with HOOC-CDs, CDs-DNA1 complex exhibited a more intensive absorption below 325nm (Fig. 2 a). Based on the absorption spectrum of DNA1, the intensive absorption should be attributed to the amide coupling of CDs with DNA1. Through specific matching of double-strands DNA with complementary base sequences, DNA2 labelled with 5Fu at the 5′-end (5Fu-DNA2) was combined with CDs-DNA1 to fabricate CDs-5Fu DNA assembly. Compared to CDs-DNA1, the DNA assembly of CDs-5Fu had an increased absorption. After the loading of Glu in the DNA double strands of CDs-5Fu via insert effects, CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid was prepared and showed a further increased absorption. Rationally, the absorption spectra of 5Fu and Glu were responsible for the increase of UV absorption spectra of CDs-5Fu and CDs-5Fu-Glu below 325nm.

When excited at 290nm, HOOC-CDs emitted the RTP with an emission peak at ∼466nm (Fig. 2b). The corresponding RTP decay curve was measured and plotted by means of multi-exponential function (Fig. S1a, in Supporting Information). The average of RTP lifetimes was about 143ms. HOOC-CDs also emitted the conventional FL, similar to the RTP. By contrast, the corresponding average FL lifetime of CDs was ∼17ns, far less than that of RTP (Fig. S1b). Usually, the CDs’ FL has a strong emission, indicating a short-lived FL with a short FL lifetime. The RTP of CDs can be explained by UV absorption spectra (Fig. 2a). The two bands located at ∼290 and 325nm were respectively assigned to π-π* and n-π* transitions of CO. The RTP excitation spectrum of CDs had a band at 300∼400nm and overlapped with CO band, revealing that the RTP might be from the CO bond on CDs (Fig. S2) [16]. The singlet and triplet states of aromatic carbonyl groups are close in energy. The spin-orbit coupling is highly efficient and is prone to intersystem crossing [3]. Reasonably, the RTP of CDs is mainly attributed to the aromatic carbonyl around CDs. Besides, the RTP of CDs is also potentially relative to the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon and graphitic structures [17].

In Fig. 2b, the FL and RTP spectra of different samples were measured and compared. Notably, the FL spectra of 5Fu and human serum (300∼600nm) partially overlapped with that of CDs. The result meant that 5Fu and human serum could interfere with the FL emission CDs. When the CDs were mixed with 5Fu in serum samples, the mixture samples would demonstrate an integrated FL emission spectrum. The FL interference from short-lived background FL and autofluorescence (e.g. 5Fu and human serum) terribly restrained the analytical accuracy and testing sensitivity of CDs FL for practical applications. However, the short-lived emissions of 5Fu and serum samples could be efficiently avoided in RTP spectra. Through delaying a detection time for ca. 0.2ms, the RTP from 5Fu and serum was detected hardly any. The RTP spectrum of CDs dispersed in serum samples containing 5Fu (mixture) exhibited a strong RTP emission without the interference from 5Fu and serum, closely similar to the FL spectrum of CDs dispersed in aqueous phase. These results further suggested that the RTP of CDs could act as an efficient time-resolved probe to determine targets, even though both the short-lived background FL and autofluorescence probably coexisted in real biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RTP spectrum responses and mechanism of products
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 2c, the CDs-DNA1 complex exhibited a marked RTP emission peaked at 466nm. After combination of CDs-DNA1 and 5Fu-DNA2 via complementary double-strands DNA assembly, the CDs-5Fu was fabricated and presented an obviously decreased RTP emission of CDs (RTP-off). The RTP-off response should originate from the PIET from CDs to 5Fu, because CDs acted as the electron donors and 5Fu was an efficient electron acceptor [12–14]. After the loading of Glu in the complementary DNA double-strands of CDs-5Fu, CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid was achieved and showed a further decreased RTP. As reported, 5Fu and Glu are two important anticancer drugs and widely employed through loading into drug delivery vehicles for tumor therapy [12–14,18–20]. Thence, CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid could act as a novel drug carrier that loaded dual-drug of 5Fu and Glu. The dual-drug loaded carriers would offer an efficient synergistic therapeutic treatment for cancer cells [21]. Also, this as-proposed novel hybrid carrier would be significant for in vitro and in vivo tumor therapy. When AFP (as one of important tumor biomarkers) was added in the hybrid-based carrier system, a remarkable “RTP-on” response of CDs was observed in Fig. 2c. As for the mechanism, AFP could specifically combine with its aptamer (i.e. DNA2) [22–25], which led to the unwinding of CDs-DNA1 with 5Fu-DNA2, together with the separation (release) of 5Fu and Glu from CDs. Since the combination of AFP with DNA2 was highly specific, the AFP-triggered 5Fu-Glu release had a high selectivity and affinity. Upon the release of 5Fu from CDs, the PIET process would be restrained and thus the RTP of CDs was recovered simultaneously. Besides, the control experiment confirmed that Glu had a slight (<5%) influence on the RTP of CDs (Fig. S3). The dramatic RTP responses almost wholly come from 5Fu.

The RTP stability and mechanism of CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid were further studied before practical applications. The hybrid was dispersed in 1mM of PBS at pH 4-10 (Fig. S4a) and incubated at 20–40°C (Fig. S4b) and the according RTP intensities of hybrid were determined respectively. The relatively high RTP intensities were detected when the hybrid was dispersed in 1mM of PBS at pH 6.5-7.4 or incubated at 25–37°C. As 37°C and pH 6.5 are close to physiological temperature of human body and the acidity of tumor microenvironment, 37°C and pH 6.5 are selected as the optimal conditions for RTP measurements and applications. When the hybrid was dispersed in 1mM of PBS at pH 6.5 and incubated at 37°C for 24h (Fig. S4c), RTP intensities of the hybrid only had a negligible decrease (<8%). These results indicated the high RTP stability of hybrid in aqueous solution. To further verify the process of AFP-triggered drug release from CDs-5Fu-Glu, the average hydrodynamic diameters of different products were measured by DLS (Fig. S4d). The average hydrodynamic diameters of HOOC-CDs, CDs-DNA1, CDs-5Fu-Glu before and after AFP addition were 19.3±1.1, 25.2±0.9, 33.8±1.6, and 26.5±1.2nm, respectively. From HOOC- CDs to CDs-DNA1, the increased diameter proved the amide coupling of HOOC-CDs and DNA1. CDs-5Fu-Glu had an increased diameter when compared with CDs-DNA1, which was attributed to the combination of CDs-DNA1 and 5Fu-DNA2 and the loading of Glu. AFP addition induced the unwinding of CDs-DNA1 with 5Fu-DNA2, causing the transfer from CDs-5Fu-Glu to CDs-DNA1. Thus, the average hydrodynamic diameter of CDs-5Fu-Glu reduced after the addition of AFP, and was very similar to that of CDs-DNA1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RTP turn-on probe of AFP and AFP-triggered drug release
ce:section.ce:para: With the increase of [AFP], RTP emission spectra of hybrid increased gradually (Fig. 2d). The relative RTP peak intensities (I/I 0) had an almost linear increment with [AFP] in the [AFP] range from 11.2nM to 1.8μM (Fig. S5a). By changing [AFP] to Log [AFP], a boarder linear range was obtained. The I/I 0 values exhibited a nearly linear increment with Log [AFP] in the [AFP] range from 11.2nM to 177.8μM (Fig. 2e, Fig. S5b). The limit of detection (LOD) of AFP was estimated to ∼3.7nM at a signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio of 3. Consequently, CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid could serve as a novel RTP probe of AFP. To evaluate the selectivity of this probe, we studied the RTP responses of hybrid in the presence of AFP, other tumor biomarkers, some ions and biomolecules from real human serum samples (Fig. 3 a,b) [14,25]. These potential interferents, at a concentration of even 150-fold higher than [AFP], only produced negligible (∼1.3) effects on the RTP of CDs. However, 178μM of AFP induced dramatic (∼11.5) RTP-on responses of CDs. These results revealed that this probe enabled the highly selective and sensitive detection of AFP, over potential interferents. Moreover, the feasibility of this probe was also investigated by testing its analytical performance in real human serum samples (Table S1). As a result, all detected values of [AFP] from this probe were in good agreement with the spiked ones, accompanying with the high detection recoveries of 95.5∼104.0%, low relative standard deviation (RSD) and narrow RSD distribution of 2.3∼4.1% (six repeated measurements). These results further suggested the superior analytical performance of this probe for AFP detection in real biological samples.

In previous papers, AFP was detected by using differential pulse voltammetry, evanescent wave absorption and mass-based piezoelectric methods (Table S2) [26–30], but the drawbacks of these methods were obvious and mainly referred to complicated manipulation, low selectivity, high cost, inferior accuracy, etc. In this work, the detection range (11.2 nM∼177.8μM) of AFP from this RTP probe was much broader than that of previous methods for AFP detection. Besides, this RTP probe had several advantages, including the use of biocompatible CDs and RTP. Significantly, the high selectivity and sensitivity of this RTP probe for AFP detection had been verified experimentally in real biological samples, with high detection recoveries and low RSD.

As for CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid, another important function was AFP-triggered release of dual-drug (5Fu and Glu). The specific binding of AFP with 5Fu-DNA2 induced the separation of CDs-DNA1 and 5Fu-DNA2. Upon the unwinding of DNA1 and DNA2, Glu loaded into complementary DNA double strands via insert effects could be released. With the addition of AFP (178μM), the release of Glu (5Fu) from 1mgmL−1 of hybrid dispersed in PBS was much rapid at the initial stage of 0∼3h (Fig. 2f). After 3h of incubation, more than 71% (51%) of Glu (5Fu) was released. By extending the incubation time from 3 to 6h, less than 3% of Glu (5Fu) release was detected. These results implied that the nearly whole release of Glu and 5Fu could be achieved after AFP addition for 3h. At a release time interval of 3h, almost whole (∼95%) RTP was recovered accordingly. The high agreement between RTP recovery and dual-drug release verified that the hybrid could be used as a novel RTP probe for AFP sensing. Meanwhile, this probe also served as a versatile drug carrier to realize AFP-triggered dual-drug release in the sensing of AFP.

As a versatile carrier, the capability of CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid for AFP-triggered dual-drug release was evaluated. After HeLa cells were incubated with CDs-5Fu for 3h, ∼90% of cell viability was still maintained, because 5Fu was combined with CDs via DNA complementary assembly without 5Fu release (Fig. 3c). Upon the addition of AFP, AFP could trigger 5Fu release, and thus the cell viability was reduced to ∼43%, attributed to the cytotoxicity of 5Fu. As for CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid, the addition of AFP led to ∼6% of cell viability. Rationally, AFP-triggered releases of 5Fu and Glu were responsible for the detected high cytotoxicity. These experimental results further confirmed that the hybrid could serve as a versatile nanocarrier used for the AFP-triggered dual-drug release and tumor therapy.

In summary, CDs-5Fu complex was facilely prepared via DNA complementary assembly. After the loading of Glu via insert effects, CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid was fabricated and developed towards a novel RTP probe for AFP sensing and a versatile carrier for AFP-triggered dual-drug release. Both applications of the hybrid were attributed to the specific binding of AFP with its aptamer DNA. As confirmed in experiments, the hybrid-based RTP probe could highly selective and sensitive detect AFP in real biological samples, over potential interferents. Significantly, upon the process of AFP sensing, AFP-triggered dual-drug release of 5Fu and Glu occurred simultaneously. Based on two important applications from the RTP sensing of tumor biomarkers and tumor biomarkers-triggered dual-drug release, the CDs-5Fu-Glu hybrid would be further developed towards a versatile carrier for highly efficient tumor diagnose and synergistic therapy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong, China (ZR2014BQ001 and ZR2016BB37), and the Source Innovation Plan Application Basic Research Project of Qingdao, China (17-1-1-72-jch).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.08.178.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Room-temperature phosphorescence logic gates developed from nucleic acid functionalized carbon dots and graphene oxide
Long lifetime pure organic phosphorescence based on water soluble carbon dots
Efficient long lifetime room temperature phosphorescence of carbon dots in a potash alum matrix
Large-scale synthesis of N-doped carbon quantum dots and their phosphorescence properties in a polyurethane matrix
Phosphate-containing metabolites switch on phosphorescence of ferric ion engineered carbon dots in aqueous solution
Efficient room-temperature phosphorescence from nitrogen-doped carbon dots in composite matrices
Synthesis of amphiphilic carbon quantum dots with phosphorescence properties and their multifunctional applications
Aptamer-based turn-on detection of thrombin in biological fluids based on efficient phosphorescence energy transfer from Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots to carbon nanodots
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning sensors, and bioimaging
A universal immunosensing strategy based on regulation of the interaction between graphene and graphene quantum dots
Manganese-doped ZnSe quantum dots as a probe for time-resolved fluorescence detection of 5-fluorouracil
Facile synthesis of quantum dots/mesoporous silica/quantum dots core/shell/shell hybrid microspheres for ratiometric fluorescence detection of 5-fluorouracil in human serum
Aptamer and 5-fluorouracil dual-loading Ag2S quantum dots used as a sensitive label-free probe for near-infrared photoluminescence turn-on detection of CA125 Antigen
Phosphorescent quantum dots/doxorubicin nanohybrids based on photoinduced electron transfer for detection of DNA
Activating efficient phosphorescence from purely organic materials by crystal design
Heavy atom induced room temperature phosphorescence: a tool for the analytical characterization of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons
Facile synthesis of gold nanorods/hydrogels core/shell nanospheres for pH and near-infrared-light induced release of 5-fluorouracil and chemo-photothermal therapy
D-glucosamine inhibits proliferation of human cancer cells through inhibition of p70S6 K
CuS nanocrystal@microgel nanocomposites for light-regulated release of dual-drugs and chemo-photothermal synergistic therapy in vitro
Ratiometric electrochemiluminescent aptasensor array for antibiotic based on internal standard method and spatial-resolved technique
Regression of hepatocarcinoma cells using RNA aptamer specific to alpha-fetoprotein
Recent advances in biosensor development for the detection of cancer biomarkers
Platinum porous nanoparticles hybrid with metal ions as probes for simultaneous detection of multiplex cancer biomarkers
Electrochemical immunosensor for simultaneous detection of multiplex cancer biomarkers based on graphene nanocomposites
Gold nanoparticle amplified optical microfiber evanescent wave absorption biosensor for cancer biomarker detection in serum
A piezoelectric immunosensor for the detection of α-fetoprotein using an interface of gold/hydroxyapatite hybrid nanomaterial
Detection of cancer biomarkers by piezoelectric biosensor using PZT ceramic resonator as the transducer

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Rijun Gui received his BS degree in 2005, and completed his MS degree in Applied Chemistry from Shanghai Ocean University in 2009. He then studied at East China University of Science and Technology to pursue a Ph.D. degree in Physical Chemistry. After completing his Ph.D. degree in 2012, he worked as a postdoctoral research fellow at Shanghai Jiao Tong University. Since 2014, he joined to Qingdao University, where he is currently an assistant professor. His main research interests focus on synthesis, properties and applications of electrochemical sensors and luminescent nanomaterials.
Weijie He was born in 1987. Since 2015, she worked as a master candidate in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University. Her research interests focus on photochemical sensors.

Hui Jin was born in 1983. She obtained her Ph.D. in Organic Chemistry from Tongji University. Since 2015, she worked as a postdoctoral research fellow at Qingdao University. Since 2017, she worked as an assistant professor in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University. Her researches focus on photo-/electro- chemical sensors and advanced nanomaterials for chemo./bio-sensing and bio-imaging.

Jie Sun received his Ph.D. from Guangzhou Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academic of Science. Currently, he works as an assistant professor in Institute of Materia Medica, Shandong Academy of Medical Sciences. His research interests focus on synthesis, properties and applications of luminescent nanomaterials.

Yanfeng Wang obtained her Ph.D. from Shandong University. Currently, she works as an assistant professor in Institute of Materia Medica, Shandong Academy of Medical Sciences. Her researches focus on the preparation and applications of luminescent nanomaterials.
